fuzzy_prd:
We need a transparent relationship-batching layer for a relational data model that groups concurrent requests and avoids repetitive round-trips to the database. The product serves backend developers who frequently load collections of linked records for multiple owner entities at once — for example, loading team memberships for a set of projects in one shot instead of one query per project. The layer must intercept the standard collection-getter calls issued within the same processing tick, fold the owner identifiers into a single batched query, and route each result set back to the correct caller. It must support an optional shared loading context that caches results so that a second wave of identical requests adds zero additional database round-trips. Beyond simple retrieval the layer must handle counting (returning how many linked records each owner has), a flag to treat an empty result as an error rather than an empty list, per-request row caps, and per-request attribute filters. Two requests can share a round-trip only if they are semantically identical in every dimension; any difference in row cap, filter, output mode, or relationship name must force separate queries. The system must also support baked-in relationship scopes — a fixed filter on the relationship itself (applied to either the linked records or the join rows) that narrows results for every load of that relationship. The batch-splitting logic lives in the internal coalescing module, and the observable proof of correct batching is captured by the query-counting instrumentation in the test adapter.

One extra detail on when things do and don’t collapse together: requests need to match on the relationship itself, so 'members' vs 'awesomeMembers' vs 'secretMembers' are separate, and they also need to match on options.limit, options.where, and options.raw. Same idea for anything else that changes the cache key from getCacheKey, since that key is built by stringifying association, attributes, groupedLimit, limit, offset, order, where, through, raw, searchPath, paranoid. So if any of those differ, they should route into different batched queries instead of sharing one.

On the shared loading context piece, when the caller passes context: true the adapter calls createContext(connection) and gets back an object with a 'loaders' map. That context gets attached to the getter options under EXPECTED_OPTIONS_KEY, which is the string 'dataloader_sequelize_context'. In that mode the DataLoader instances use cache: true, which is why a second wave of identical loads can come back with zero additional SELECT queries. If there isn’t a context, we still reuse DataLoader instances through the global LRU cache, meaning GLOBAL_CACHE, max 500 entries, but those loaders still run with cache: false.

Also worth calling out how the empty-result behavior works. If options.rejectOnEmpty is truthy and the result array is empty according to lodash isEmpty, then the rejectOnEmpty function throws a Sequelize.EmptyResultError when rejectOnEmpty is exactly true, or calls the value as a constructor if it is a function, or throws it directly if it is an object. The adapter then catches anything whose name is 'SequelizeEmptyResultError' or matches /EmptyResult/ and turns that into the string 'error=empty_result'. Even with that flag set, those requests still share the same batched query as other requests for the same owner/relationship, because rejectOnEmpty is stripped from findOptions before model.findAll is called.

For limit handling, if options.limit is set and the batch has more than one key, we switch over to groupedLimit instead of doing a plain limit across the whole batch. In the BelongsToMany case that becomes options.groupedLimit = { through: options.through, on: association, limit: findOptions.limit, values: uniq(keys) }. For non-BTM hasMany it becomes { limit: findOptions.limit, on: attributeField, values: uniq(keys) }, and then the limit key is deleted. If there’s only one key in the batch, we just use the normal WHERE + LIMIT path. And since different limit values produce different cache keys, requests with different limits won’t share a DataLoader anyway, so they always split into separate batched queries.

If anyone is looking for the exact implementation surface, this all lives in golden/src/index.js — the core library file that contains the DataLoader-based batching logic, getCacheKey, shimBelongsToMany, and all other shim functions.

trigger_keywords: how does a join-scoped relationship filter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In shimBelongsToMany, if this.through.scope is set, it is merged into options.through.where using Sequelize.Op.and (or '$and' for older Sequelize versions). The resulting options.through.where is then passed into the include or groupedLimit.through object in the findAll call. This means the join table rows are pre-filtered by the scope condition (e.g. { secret: true }) before the linked records are returned. The through.scope is part of the cache key via the 'through' field in getCacheKey, so a join-scoped relationship always gets a separate DataLoader from an unscoped one.

trigger_keywords: what does the default export accept
what argument does the main function take
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default export function(target, options = {}) accepts three kinds of targets: (1) an individual association object (target.associationType is set) — shimAssociation is called on it; (2) a Sequelize Model class — shimModel is called on it and then shimAssociation is called on each of its associations; (3) the Sequelize constructor itself — shimModel is called on the Model prototype and shimBelongsTo/HasOne/HasMany/BelongsToMany are called on their respective Association prototypes. options.max (default 500) controls the LRU cache size. The function also initialises the GLOBAL_CACHE LRU if it has not been created yet.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for batch lines
how are batch lines sorted in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The first output line is 'queries=<N>' where N is the total number of model.findAll calls intercepted. Then one 'batch=<keys>' line per findAll, where keys are the owner ids converted to numbers and sorted ascending (a - b), joined by commas. The batch lines themselves are sorted lexicographically before output so their order does not depend on query completion order. Then for each round r (0-indexed) and each op index i (0-indexed), one line 'r<r>.op<i>=<payload>'. Payload is 'members=<sorted ids>' for a get (empty string after '=' for an empty collection), 'count=<n>' for a count op, or 'error=<category>' for a caught exception.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the query-counting instrumentation in the test adapter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: rcb_tests/dispatcher/run.js — the execution adapter that wraps env.User.findAll to intercept calls, collects the batched key sets via batchKeysOf(options), and renders the 'queries=' / 'batch=' / 'r<r>.op<i>=' output lines

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match any documented constraint. Please refer to the core batching logic in golden/src/index.js (especially getCacheKey, loaderForBTM, shimBelongsToMany) and the adapter in rcb_tests/dispatcher/run.js for authoritative behaviour.

feature1_1_batched_load.json.description: Several owner records each need the collection of peer records linked to them through a join table. The owner-side collection getters are invoked for multiple owners within the same execution tick, without awaiting in between. Instead of issuing one underlying SELECT per owner, the loader folds all of those requests into a single batched round-trip whose filter carries the full set of owner keys, then de-multiplexes the rows back to each owner. The reported signals are the number of underlying round-trips actually issued, the owner-key set folded into each round-trip, and the linked-record id set returned to each owner. The join table can be declared either by name or as an explicit model; behavior is identical.

feature1_2_cached_load.json.description: A caller obtains a shared loading context and threads it through every collection load. The first wave of concurrent loads is satisfied by one batched round-trip; a second wave that repeats the exact same loads is served entirely from the context's cache and triggers no further round-trips. The reported signals are the total number of underlying round-trips across both waves (which stays at the single initial batch) and the linked-record id set returned to each owner in each wave (identical across waves).

feature2_batched_count.json.description: Several owners each need the COUNT of peer records linked to them through the join table, requested concurrently. The loader batches all the counts into a single aggregated round-trip and returns the per-owner total, with an owner that has no linked records reporting a count of zero. The reported signals are the number of underlying round-trips, the owner-key set folded into the batch, and the numeric count returned for each owner.

feature3_reject_on_empty.json.description: A load may request that an empty result be treated as an error rather than an empty collection. When such a load resolves to no linked records it is rejected with a neutral empty-result error category; an owner that does have linked records resolves normally; and the same empty owner loaded WITHOUT the reject flag resolves to an empty collection. All of these are issued concurrently and still share a single batched round-trip. The reported signals are the round-trip count and, per load, either the returned id set, an empty collection, or the empty-result error category.

feature4_limit_batching.json.description: When loads carry a per-owner row cap (limit), owners that share the same cap can still be batched together, but owners requesting a different cap cannot share a round-trip with them. So a wave of loads with mixed caps is split into one batched round-trip per distinct cap, each round-trip carrying only the owner keys that share that cap, and each owner receives at most `limit` linked records (the lowest-keyed ones). The reported signals are the number of round-trips, the owner-key set per round-trip, and the capped id set returned to each owner.

cases.expected_output:
queries=2
batch=1
batch=2,3
r0.op0=members=101,102,103,104
r0.op1=members=104,105
r0.op2=members=107,108

feature5_filter_batching.json.description: Loads may carry a per-request filter on the linked record's attributes. Only loads whose filter (and any row cap) match can be batched together; loads with different filters are routed into separate round-trips. Each owner then receives only its linked records that satisfy that load's filter, capped if a limit was supplied. The reported signals are the number of round-trips, the owner-key set folded into each round-trip, and the filtered (and capped) id set returned to each owner.

feature6_relationship_scope.json.description: A relationship may be declared with a built-in scope, i.e. a fixed filter baked into the relationship itself so that loading it only ever returns linked records matching that filter. Loads of a scoped relationship batch together (one round-trip) just like an unscoped one and honor row caps the same way (mixed caps split into separate round-trips). A scoped load may also be requested in raw mode, which cannot share a round-trip with a non-raw load. Two DIFFERENT relationships between the same pair of records (e.g. the scoped one and the unscoped one) never share a round-trip. The reported signals are the round-trip count, the owner-key set per round-trip, and the id set returned to each owner (raw rows are reported by their ids just like hydrated records).

cases.expected_output:
queries=2
batch=1,2
batch=3
r0.op0=members=102,103
r0.op1=members=105,107
r0.op2=members=107,108

feature7_join_scope.json.description: A relationship may instead carry a scope on the join table itself, i.e. a fixed filter applied to the link rows so that only links flagged a certain way connect the two records. Loads of a join-scoped relationship batch together into a single round-trip and honor row caps the same way (mixed caps split into separate round-trips). A join-scoped relationship and the plain relationship between the same pair never share a round-trip. The reported signals are the round-trip count, the owner-key set per round-trip, and the id set returned to each owner.

cases.expected_output:
queries=2
batch=1,2
batch=3
r0.op0=members=101,102
r0.op1=members=105
r0.op2=members=107,108
